source: Furness, New Variorum Hamlet (1877) — line-keyed notes. Demo excerpt; full parse via scripts/ingest-criticism.ts.

notes.lemma: A little more than kin, and less than kind
notes.note: Furness collects the gloss war: 'kind' as both 'kindred/kinsman' and 'natural affection/nature.' Hamlet is more than kin (uncle now father) and less than kind (neither natural in feeling nor of a kindly disposition). Johnson: 'less than kind' = not of the same nature or character.

The great crux. Q2 reads 'sallied,' F reads 'solid.' Furness prints the divergence at length; nineteenth-century editors overwhelmingly followed F's 'solid'; the twentieth century (Dover Wilson) recovered 'sullied' from 'sallied.' See refusal-map: sullied-solid.

Furness notes the line has been read both as a universal (mankind's frailty figured as woman) and as a specific indictment of Gertrude. The generalization is Hamlet's, not the play's; commentators warn against taking the hero's misogyny as the drama's verdict.
